deflection: That is outside the realm of alchemy. I deal in reactions, not that.
Interesting question, but not one I can answer with a beaker.
My expertise is chemical, not... whatever that is. Ask about potions.
greetings: Ah, {player_name}! Come in, come in! Mind the bubbling flask on the left.
*peers over spectacles* Yes? Oh! A customer! Welcome to my laboratory!
Perfect timing! I just finished a batch of... well, never mind what. How can I help?
Welcome! Do not touch anything green. Or glowing. Especially if it is both.
A {player_race}! Fascinating physiology. I mean -- welcome!
farewells: Safe travels! And if anything I sold you starts smoking, set it down gently.
Come back anytime! I am always brewing something new.
Farewell, {player_name}! Remember: never mix red and blue vials. Trust me.
thanks_responses: You are welcome! Science serves all who seek it!
No need for thanks! Your coin is thanks enough. And your continued survival.
insult_responses: How rude! I could turn you into a toad. I probably will not. Probably.
Insults? I have been called worse by my own failed experiments.
*scribbles in notebook* Fascinating aggression. Is this a chronic condition?
no_match_responses: Hmm? Sorry, I was distilling. What did you say?
I did not catch that. Was it about potions, ingredients, or experiments?
You have lost me, and I was already lost in a formula. Try again?
Not sure what you mean, {player_name}. Alchemy questions are my strength.

responses.text: Potions are my pride and joy! Healing draughts, stamina tonics, antidotes -- I brew them all.
A good potion is part science, part art, and a tiny part luck. The tiny part is what makes it exciting.
My healing potions are the best in {town}. The healer buys from me, if that tells you anything.
I just perfected a new stamina elixir. Keeps you running for hours. Side effects are... minimal.
You asked about potions already. The selection has not changed in the last few minutes.
Good ingredients make good potions. I source herbs from the forest and minerals from the mines.
Moonpetal flowers are rare but essential for night vision elixirs. I pay well for them.
The quality of ingredients matters enormously. Stale herbs produce weak or unpredictable results.
I am always buying rare ingredients. If you find anything unusual in your travels, bring it here.
Same ingredients as before. Botany does not change overnight, {player_name}.
I am currently researching the properties of cave moss. Bioluminescence has alchemical potential!
My last experiment created a substance that dissolves wood but not stone. Applications pending.
Science requires failure! I have failed magnificently at least twenty times this month.
*gestures excitedly* See that stain on the ceiling? That was last week's experiment. Great success. Mostly.
Experiments continue as always. Science does not rest, and neither do I!
Transmutation! The great dream. Turning lead to gold. I am... closer than most think.
True transmutation requires understanding the fundamental elements. We are decades away. Maybe years.
Everyone asks about gold. Nobody asks about the real breakthroughs -- purifying water, enriching soil.
I turned copper green once. That counts. Do not argue with me about what counts.
Still no gold from lead. But I am making progress on tin to silver. Close enough?
The classical elements -- fire, water, earth, air -- are useful models but incomplete.
Sulfur, mercury, and salt. The tria prima. The foundation of all alchemical theory.
Every substance is a combination of elemental essences. Understanding the ratio is the key to alchemy.
Fire is the most useful element in alchemy. And the most dangerous. My eyebrows agree.
I keep my best recipes locked away. Trade secrets, you understand. But basics I will share freely.
A healing potion is simple: moonpetal, spring water, and a pinch of salt. Boil, do not simmer.
The difference between a potion and a poison is often just the dosage. Remember that.
I have recipes passed down from master alchemists. Some are centuries old and still effective.
Reagents are the active components that drive reactions. Without them, you just have expensive soup.
I import crystallized moonstone from the dwarven mines. Expensive but irreplaceable.
Some reagents are volatile. I lost a table last month. And a wall. The wall was mostly intact.
If you find any glowing crystals in dungeons, bring them to me. I pay very well for those.
Explosions are uncontrolled reactions. I prefer controlled ones. Usually. Sometimes they surprise me.
I have blown out three windows this year. The glazier gives me a bulk discount now.
Volatile compounds demand respect. Mix sulfur and saltpeter carelessly and you redecorate the room.
Every alchemist has explosion stories. The ones who do not are the ones who did not survive.
No new explosions since you asked. Give me an hour.
Rare reagents! Phoenix ash, dragon scale dust, essence of starlight. I have seen them all. Briefly.
The rarest reagent I own is a vial of tears from a weeping willow spirit. Do not ask how I got it.
If you ever find heartstone -- a gem that pulses like a heartbeat -- bring it to me. Name your price.
Rare materials make impossible potions possible. But they are called rare for a reason.
